findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify and correct the assertion that `acli_graph._RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK` is the canonical source: Grep confirms it does not exist in `acli_graph.py`; update the plan to describe the `outbound_links.py` local copy as the ONLY existing definition (with no extant counterpart in `acli_graph`), and explicitly call out that the 'single-source' step under `adapters/jira/` must CREATE a new canonical constant rather than consolidate two copies.
evidence: Plan text: 'outbound_links.py:30-54 — a hand-copied duplicate of acli_graph._RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK ("keep in sync with acli_graph") — the duplicated link-constant leak.'
Grep of acli_graph.py for '_RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK': no matches
Grep of acli_graph.py for 'RELATION': no matches
Grep of acli_graph.py for 'Blocks': no matches
outbound_links.py:30-35 comment: 'The canonical definition lives in acli_graph._RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK (Cycle 1)' — this is a stale comment in the source file itself, not current fact
findings.finding: The plan asserts `outbound_links.py:30-54` is 'a hand-copied duplicate of `acli_graph._RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK`' — but `_RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK` does not exist anywhere in `acli_graph.py`. Grepping the file finds zero matches for `_RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK`, `RELATION`, or `Blocks`. The stale comment inside `outbound_links.py` itself calls this the canonical source '(Cycle 1)', but the symbol was evidently removed or never landed there. This means: (a) there is no extant canonical single-sourced copy to point to; (b) the relocate step for this leak site must CREATE a new canonical definition in `adapters/jira/`, not merge a duplicate; (c) the neutrality AC's assertion that 'outbound_links link constants are single-sourced under adapters/jira/' and 'grepping the core sites for those constructs returns zero hits' may behave unexpectedly if the implementer searches for a symbol that doesn't exist in the place the plan describes.
findings.location: Context / Problem — 'outbound_links.py:30-54 — a hand-copied duplicate of acli_graph._RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK'
scenarios: Implementer searches acli_graph.py for _RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK to understand what to consolidate, finds nothing, and either skips the consolidation or invents an incorrect canonical source.
The neutrality AC's grep targeting acli_graph._RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK passes vacuously (symbol absent) while the real duplicate in outbound_links.py remains unrelocated.

The 'single-sourcing' step in Scope is scoped as relocating an existing duplicate into adapters/jira/, but since no canonical source exists, a create-from-scratch step is actually needed — this is unacknowledged work.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace: 'a hand-copied duplicate of acli_graph._RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK ("keep in sync with acli_graph")' with: 'the only current definition of the relation→Jira-link-type vocabulary (the stale comment claims `acli_graph._RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK` is the canonical source, but that symbol does not exist in acli_graph.py — the constant in outbound_links.py IS the sole definition). The relocation step must move this definition into adapters/jira/ and have outbound_links.py import from there.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate and update all test/external callers that access vendor symbols via `outbound_differ.<name>` re-exports (confirmed: `test_outbound_differ_session_log_exclusion.py:85`, `test_reconcile_live_e2e.py:531/561/698`, `test_preflight_status_dict.py:145`) before removing those re-export imports.
evidence: Plan says: 'outbound_differ.py:63 — from rebar_reconciler.outbound_fields import _map_local_to_jira_fields, _diff_fields'
Actual file: lines 63-69 import _LOCAL_TO_JIRA_TYPE, _assignee_matches, _diff_fields, _extract_jira_field, _map_local_to_jira_fields all with # noqa: F401 (re-export intent)
test_outbound_differ_session_log_exclusion.py:85: assert 'session_log' not in outbound_differ._LOCAL_TO_JIRA_TYPE
test_reconcile_live_e2e.py:531: changed = outbound_differ._diff_fields(...)
test_preflight_status_dict.py:145: outbound_differ._map_local_to_jira_fields
findings.finding: The plan describes the outbound_differ.py:63 leak site as importing only `_map_local_to_jira_fields, _diff_fields`, but the actual import block (lines 63-69) re-exports five symbols via `# noqa: F401`: `_LOCAL_TO_JIRA_TYPE`, `_assignee_matches`, `_diff_fields`, `_extract_jira_field`, and `_map_local_to_jira_fields`. Several test files OUTSIDE core access these via `outbound_differ.<name>` (e.g., `test_outbound_differ_session_log_exclusion.py:85` asserts `outbound_differ._LOCAL_TO_JIRA_TYPE`, `test_reconcile_live_e2e.py:531/561/698` calls `outbound_differ._diff_fields`, `test_preflight_status_dict.py:145` uses `outbound_differ._map_local_to_jira_fields`). These callers are unenumerated update targets: if the re-exports are removed to satisfy the neutrality gate, these tests break.
findings.location: Context / Problem (leak sites); outbound_differ.py:63-69; test files: test_outbound_differ_session_log_exclusion.py:85, test_reconcile_live_e2e.py:531/561/698, test_preflight_status_dict.py:145
scenarios: The neutrality gate asserts zero vendor imports in rewired core modules; deleting the outbound_differ.py import block removes the re-exports; external test consumers of outbound_differ._LOCAL_TO_JIRA_TYPE/_diff_fields/_map_local_to_jira_fields fail with AttributeError.
findings.suggested_fix: Add these files to the plan's edit-target list; either redirect them to the adapter (e.g., `adapters.jira.jira_fields._LOCAL_TO_JIRA_TYPE`) or keep shim re-exports in outbound_differ.py that forward from the adapter, deferring full re-export removal to S5.

findings.finding: The plan's description of the outbound_differ.py:63 leak site lists only `_map_local_to_jira_fields, _diff_fields`, but the actual import at line 63 also re-exports `_LOCAL_TO_JIRA_TYPE`, `_assignee_matches`, and `_extract_jira_field` with `# noqa: F401`. These additional re-exported vendor symbols are not mentioned in the scope, leak-site inventory, or AC, so the neutrality gate (AC #1) and the rewire scope may silently miss them.
findings.location: Context / Problem — 'Confirmed leak sites' / outbound_differ.py:63; AC #1
findings.suggested_fix: Add these three names to the leak-site inventory and AC #1's grep pattern, or add a separate AC bullet explicitly permitting the re-exports with a rationale (e.g. 'test-suite backward-compat shim, removed in S5').

findings.finding: The plan asserts '23 test files use that seam' (the `_load_acli` patch.object seam) to scope the test-update work, but a grep confirms at least 33 distinct test files patch `_load_acli`, including several integration test files not under `tests/unit/`. The undercount could cause the implementer to miss updating integration tests in the lockstep change.
findings.location: Approach section — '23 test files use that seam — they inject types.SimpleNamespace(AcliClient=lambda **_: mock_client)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace '23 test files' with the actual count from a fresh grep and add a note that integration tests in tests/integration/rebar_reconciler/ use a monkeypatch/setattr pattern rather than patch.object and need separate handling.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `apply_handlers.py` as an explicit edit target for the `_load_acli` seam contract change, specifying how `ctx.acli.AssigneeNotFoundError` will continue to resolve after `_load_acli` is evolved to yield the backend transport.
evidence: apply_handlers.py:69: `acli: Any` field on BatchApplyContext
apply_handlers.py:181: `except ctx.acli.AssigneeNotFoundError as exc:`
applier.py:654-656: ctx = BatchApplyContext(client=client, acli=acli, ...) where acli comes from _load_acli()
Plan: 'evolve the seam + its patch.object tests' — apply_handlers.py not listed
findings.finding: `apply_handlers.py:181` is an unenumerated consumer of the `_load_acli` seam contract change. It catches `ctx.acli.AssigneeNotFoundError`, where `ctx.acli` is the value returned by `applier._load_acli()` threaded through `BatchApplyContext`. The plan evolves `applier._load_acli` to 'yield the backend transport' but never names `apply_handlers.py` as an edit target, even though changing the shape of what `_load_acli` returns directly changes what `ctx.acli` is and whether `ctx.acli.AssigneeNotFoundError` resolves.
findings.location: Approach section (applier/fetcher _load_acli seam evolution); apply_handlers.py:69,181; applier.py:654-656
scenarios: If _load_acli is evolved to return a TicketTransport (which has no AssigneeNotFoundError attribute), the except clause at apply_handlers.py:181 raises AttributeError at runtime when an assignee is unresolvable.
Even if ctx.acli is kept as the acli module for exception lookup, the plan must explicitly acknowledge and handle this dependency.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly address how `ctx.acli.AssigneeNotFoundError` at `apply_handlers.py:181` is resolved after the `_load_acli` seam stops returning the acli module — either by moving the exception class to `_backend.py`/`TicketTransport`, surfacing it on the `Backend` protocol, or documenting how the new seam shape still makes the exception accessible; add a targeted test that triggers this exception path through the new seam.
evidence: Plan states: 'evolve the seam + its `patch.object` tests in the same change (23 test files use that seam — they inject `types.SimpleNamespace(AcliClient=lambda **_: mock_client)`; the seam keeps returning something whose transport those doubles satisfy)'
apply_handlers.py:181: `except ctx.acli.AssigneeNotFoundError as exc:` — uses ctx.acli as both a transport handle AND as the source of the exception class
The plan's AC verifies seam tests pass and characterization suite is green, but neither explicitly names the AssigneeNotFoundError catch-site as a verification target
Prior review cache (.rebar/cache/plan-review/6673-7636-a116-4f90/a2304c4ce164b37e9fb2.json) has flagged this same gap; the plan text still does not address it
findings.finding: The plan asserts that evolving the `_load_acli` seam to return a `TicketTransport` (instead of the acli module) is test-compatible and clean, but does not address `apply_handlers.py:181`'s `except ctx.acli.AssigneeNotFoundError as exc:` — an exception-class attribute lookup on `ctx.acli` that will break when the seam stops returning the module. After the rewire, `ctx.acli` is no longer the acli module and does not expose `AssigneeNotFoundError`, so this `except` clause will either raise `AttributeError` at runtime or silently fail to catch the exception, allowing `AssigneeNotFoundError` to propagate as an unhandled error.
findings.location: Approach section ('evolve the seam + its `patch.object` tests') + apply_handlers.py:181 (`except ctx.acli.AssigneeNotFoundError as exc`)
scenarios: At runtime, after the seam evolution, any outbound-update that triggers an assignee-not-found Jira error will propagate `AssigneeNotFoundError` unhandled (bypassing the soft-fail alert-store path) because the `except ctx.acli.AssigneeNotFoundError` clause can no longer resolve the exception class on the new transport object.

Tests that inject `types.SimpleNamespace(AcliClient=lambda **_: mock_client)` without an `AssigneeNotFoundError` attribute will cause the `except` clause to raise `AttributeError` when reached, masking the real behavior gap in unit tests.
findings.suggested_fix: Define `AssigneeNotFoundError` as a first-class exception on the `Backend` protocol (or its `TicketTransport`) so `ctx.acli.AssigneeNotFoundError` becomes `ctx.backend.transport.AssigneeNotFoundError` (or `ctx.backend.AssigneeNotFoundError`). Update `apply_handlers.py:181` and `outbound_assignee.py:113` in lockstep. Add a dedicated test that injects a transport mock carrying the exception class and confirms the soft-fail path fires.

findings.finding: The plan's `_load_acli` seam evolution — 'yielding the backend transport' — breaks the `ctx.acli.AssigneeNotFoundError` exception-class catch site in `apply_handlers.py:181`. Today `ctx.acli` is the module, carrying both the `AcliClient` factory and exception classes (`AssigneeNotFoundError`). A `TicketTransport` instance cannot carry exception classes without explicit protocol-level plumbing that the plan does not specify, creating a silent breakage path the characterization suite may not catch (exception-class catch sites are exercised only by specific error-path tests, not the happy-path reconcile characterization).
findings.location: Approach section / 'transport/construction sites' paragraph; `apply_handlers.py:181` (`except ctx.acli.AssigneeNotFoundError`); AC line 4 (`_load_acli seams and their 23 patch.object tests pass`)
findings.suggested_fix: Either add an `exceptions` namespace or a typed `assignee_not_found_error` attribute to `TicketTransport`, or specify that `BatchApplyContext.acli` is kept as the module (separate from the transport) and only the `client` field is sourced from the backend — making the exception-class resolution an explicit concern in the seam contract.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the neutrality grep/AC to also assert that `inbound_differ.py` no longer directly calls `inbound_fields._map_jira_to_local_fields` (e.g. add `inbound_fields` to the forbidden-import list for that module, or assert the call goes through `backend.inbound_mapper.map_fields()`), since `inbound_fields` is not covered by the current grep pattern.
evidence: inbound_differ.py:40: `from rebar_reconciler.inbound_fields import ... _map_jira_to_local_fields`
inbound_fields.py is at src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/inbound_fields.py — a sibling core module, not adapters.jira.* and not in the neutrality grep's named list
AC text: 'None of the 12 rewired core modules import `adapters.jira.*` or the root vendor modules (`outbound_fields`/`adf`/`comment_limits`/`acli*`)' — inbound_fields is not in this list
Scope says: 'Route the 8 core importer sites + 4 transport/construction sites above through the Backend port'
findings.finding: The plan lists `inbound_differ.py:141` as a core module importing the 'inbound mapper' — but the actual import is `from rebar_reconciler.inbound_fields import _map_jira_to_local_fields` (line 40), where `inbound_fields` is a sibling core module, not an `adapters.jira.*` module nor any of the named 'root vendor modules' (`outbound_fields`/`adf`/`comment_limits`/`acli*`). The Acceptance Criteria neutrality grep is defined as 'none of the 12 rewired core modules import `adapters.jira.*` or the root vendor modules (`outbound_fields`/`adf`/`comment_limits`/`acli*`)'. Under that gate, `inbound_differ.py` importing from `inbound_fields` currently passes the gate even before any rewire — but the plan counts it as a leak site to fix. If the fix is to route via the `Backend` mapper, but the gate only checks for the named root-vendor imports, the gate will not verify that the inbound_differ rewire actually happened.
findings.location: Context / Problem — 'inbound_differ.py:141 — _map_jira_to_local_fields(...) (inbound mapper)'; AC bullet 1 neutrality grep
scenarios: The neutrality grep passes (inbound_fields is not in the grep list) even if the inbound_differ rewire is never performed, because the current import from inbound_fields already passes the gate.
Implementer rewires 11 of 12 sites correctly but skips inbound_differ because the green gate doesn't cover it, and the gap goes unnoticed until a second backend is attempted.

evidence: 1 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the new `_load_acli` seam return type and the updated call-site pattern explicitly (e.g. 'seam returns a `TicketTransport`; callers use it directly as `client = _load_acli()`; test doubles become `mock_transport` objects implementing `TicketTransport`') so the 23-file test update has a clear target.
evidence: applier.py:439: `acli_mod = _load_acli()` then `:443: client = acli_mod.AcliClient(jira_url=_s.url, user=_s.user, api_token=_s.api_token)`
Plan: 'the seam keeps returning something whose transport those doubles satisfy' — no specification of the new return type or call-site shape

23 test files inject `types.SimpleNamespace(AcliClient=lambda **_: mock_client)` — all will need updating if the seam return type changes, but the plan only says 'update their tests in the same change' without specifying what the new test-double shape is
findings.finding: The plan's description of how the `applier`/`fetcher` `_load_acli` seam 'evolves to yield the backend transport' is mechanistically ambiguous: the current callers do `acli_mod = _load_acli(); client = acli_mod.AcliClient(jira_url=..., ...)` — so the seam returns a MODULE-shaped object, not a transport. The plan says the seam 'keeps returning something whose transport those doubles satisfy', but doesn't specify the new call site shape after rewiring. If the seam now returns the transport directly, EVERY call site must stop calling `.AcliClient(...)` on it. If the seam returns a Backend whose transport is accessed via `.transport`, every test stub must change shape. The plan commits to updating tests 'in lockstep' but the new seam contract (what it returns and how callers use it) is not stated, leaving this a critical implementation-time ambiguity for a 23-file change.
findings.location: Approach section — '(1) applier/fetcher — evolve their existing _load_acli seams to yield the backend transport and update their patch.object(..., "_load_acli") tests in the same change'
scenarios: Implementer changes the seam to return a Backend, forgetting that applier.py:443 still calls `.AcliClient(...)` on the result — runtime AttributeError on the transport object
Implementer changes call sites but uses `.transport` while tests still inject a SimpleNamespace without a `.transport` attribute — 23 tests fail silently or with confusing errors

findings.finding: outbound_differ.py re-exports several vendor names (_LOCAL_TO_JIRA_TYPE, _assignee_matches, _diff_fields, _extract_jira_field, _map_local_to_jira_fields) via `# noqa: F401` imports from outbound_fields (lines 63–69). At least one test (test_outbound_differ_session_log_exclusion.py:85) accesses outbound_differ._LOCAL_TO_JIRA_TYPE directly via the module object. Removing these imports to satisfy the neutrality gate ('none of the 12 rewired core modules import outbound_fields') will break those external test consumers, but the plan does not enumerate them as requiring updates.
findings.location: Confirmed leak sites / outbound_differ.py:63; AC bullet 1 (neutrality gate)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in Scope/Approach whether this story creates new adapter-layer methods in adapters/jira/ to house the inlined logic (a code-writing step), or whether those methods already exist and only the call-routing changes — and reconcile this with the 'not where files live' statement in Context.
evidence: Context: 'It changes who calls what, not where files live (physical relocation is S5).'
Scope: 'Relocate ADF-sizing, lossy status/parent, duplicated value-maps, and the outbound_links link-constant duplicate into adapters/jira/ (single-sourced).'
AC2: 'the assertion is core-emptiness + single-sourcing, path-agnostic — the file move itself is S5'
findings.finding: Context states 'It changes who calls what, not where files live (physical relocation is S5)' but Scope says 'Relocate ADF-sizing, lossy status/parent, duplicated value-maps, and the outbound_links link-constant duplicate into adapters/jira/' — AC2 then hedges with 'path-agnostic — the file move itself is S5.' An implementer cannot tell whether this story requires writing new code into adapters/jira/ (creating or extending adapter modules with the inlined logic) or only rewires calls to adapter code that already exists there.
findings.location: Context paragraph 1 vs. Scope bullet 2 vs. AC2
scenarios: Implementer writes new adapter logic in adapters/jira/ (treating 'Relocate' literally) and discovers it conflicts with what S5 was supposed to do — duplicated effort or merge conflict.

Implementer assumes the logic already lives in adapters/jira/ and only rewires calls, but it does not yet exist there — the rewired core calls a non-existent adapter method and the story is incomplete.
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to Approach: 'Where the inlined logic (ADF size-fitting, lossy rules, link constants) does not yet exist as an adapter method, this story adds it to the appropriate adapters/jira/ module as new code (single-sourcing it there for the first time); the physical relocation of the originating root-vendor files remains S5.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one end-to-end (non-mock) acceptance criterion that exercises the full `select_backend(config).transport` construction path for at least one rewired site (e.g., a test that calls `select_backend(config)` with a real config fixture and asserts the returned transport has the expected type/attributes), so the transport cutover cannot close green while the live construction path is broken.
evidence: '23 test files use that seam — they inject `types.SimpleNamespace(AcliClient=lambda **_: mock_client)`; the seam keeps returning something whose transport those doubles satisfy'
'add a fresh test that patches the new seam (there is no existing patch target to reuse)'
No live/integration acceptance criterion is stated for the transport path going through `select_backend(config).transport` end-to-end
The rubric fires proxy-validation as MAJOR when 'a cutover that defaults to a new path' is 'validated SOLELY through a mock/offline substitute that bypasses the new behavior'
findings.finding: The transport cutover defaults to `select_backend(config).transport` for all 4 construction sites (applier, fetcher, run_differs, _attestation), but every test specified (the 23 existing `patch.object(_load_acli)` tests, the new run_differs seam test, and the _attestation path update) patches/mocks the seam and never exercises `select_backend(config).transport` actually constructing and wiring a real backend transport end-to-end. No live or end-to-end acceptance criterion is stated for the defaulted transport path.
findings.location: Testing section — 'evolve the seam + its `patch.object` tests'; AC bullet 4 — 'all verified by `make test`'; Approach — '(2) run_differs … add a fresh test that patches the new seam'
scenarios: select_backend(config).transport construction has a wiring bug (wrong env-var key, wrong attribute name on the transport object) that all 23+1 mock tests are blind to — the story closes green, production transport construction is broken
_attestation.py routes through the backend transport but the construction path is wrong; offline test passes because it patches before construction happens
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC: 'A non-mock integration test constructs a Jira backend via `select_backend(config_fixture)` and asserts `backend.transport` is a `TicketTransport` instance with non-None credentials — covering at least one of the rewired sites (applier or fetcher) end-to-end without patching `_load_acli`.' This does not require a live Jira server — a config fixture with test credentials suffices to exercise the construction path.

findings.finding: The plan groups all 4 transport/construction sites under the '_load_acli seam' narrative and says '23 test files use that seam — they inject types.SimpleNamespace(AcliClient=lambda **_: mock_client)'. However, run_differs.py:403 and _attestation.py:82 do NOT have a _load_acli() seam function — they perform bare inline AcliClient() construction with no corresponding patch.object(_..., '_load_acli') test seam in the current test suite. No tests for patching run_differs or _attestation AcliClient construction are mentioned or scheduled.
findings.location: Approach section ('obtain the client from select_backend(config)'s TicketTransport and update the _load_acli seams'); Testing / AC bullet 4 ('_load_acli seams and their 23 patch.object tests')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a back-out plan or expand-contract sequence for the _load_acli seam change: either describe a feature-flag/adapter shim that keeps both shapes working during migration (expand), then a cleanup commit (contract), or explicitly state that the atomic commit is the rollback unit and all 23 files must be updated in one PR with CI as the gate.

No rollback path or expand-contract sequence is described — if the migration is partial or fails mid-implementation, the repository is in a broken intermediate state with no described back-out.

scenarios: A developer gets halfway through updating the 23 test files and needs to pause — the branch is non-green with no safe intermediate checkpoint.
The seam return type changes but a subset of test doubles still inject the old SimpleNamespace shape, causing silent test failures that are hard to bisect.

'23 test files use that seam — they inject `types.SimpleNamespace(AcliClient=lambda **_: mock_client)`' — seam contract changes but no contract document referenced

findings.finding: The plan explicitly acknowledges the existing ADR is stale ('the ADR §(a) list is stale — it names `baseline_shadow.py`, which does not exist, and omits `apply_inbound.py`') but does not include updating the ADR or any architecture doc in scope; additionally, the new run_differs seam contract (no existing patch target, different injection pattern from the 23 applier/fetcher test files) and the RemoteRef/IdentityConvention convention replacement are not documented anywhere.
findings.location: Context/Problem section (ADR §(a) stale acknowledgment); Scope section (out-of-scope list); Approach section (run_differs new seam)
scenarios: A developer adding a fifth transport site in a future story consults the stale ADR and misses the correct pattern
A maintainer looking at run_differs tests sees a different seam pattern from the other 23 files with no explanation of why
The stale ADR continues to reference the non-existent baseline_shadow.py, misleading future reviewers of the architecture
findings.suggested_fix: Add a task: 'Update ADR §(a) to remove baseline_shadow.py, add apply_inbound.py, and add a §(seams) section describing (1) the evolved _load_acli pattern for applier/fetcher and (2) the new backend-transport injection seam for run_differs, with a note that the two shapes differ because run_differs lacked a pre-existing seam.' This can be a one-paragraph addendum to the existing ADR rather than a new document.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Demote the characterization suite from 'primary guard' to a regression harness, and elevate the unit tests (ADF truncation at limit, lossy-status boundary, RemoteRef stamping, link-constant correctness) to be the primary correctness gate for the relocated logic — each unit test should assert a *spec-derived* expected value, not a value derived from current `main` output.
evidence: 'produces identical events/outcomes to pre-story `main` — the primary behavior-preserving guard'
The scope explicitly includes relocating ADF size-fitting, lossy status/parent mapping, duplicated value-maps — logic whose correctness is NOT asserted by a same-output-as-main characterization test
The rubric flags 'snapshot-of-current-output … (MAJOR — locks in the bug)' when it is the primary guard
findings.finding: The characterization test is specified as 'the primary behavior-preserving guard' and is defined as 'produces identical events/outcomes to pre-story `main`' — a snapshot of current output. Because this story also relocates Jira-specific logic (ADF size-fitting, lossy status/parent mapping, value-maps), the primary guard snapshots potentially-wrong current behavior as the oracle. Any bug in ADF truncation, lossy-status logic, or link constants that pre-exists on `main` will be preserved and locked in by the characterization suite, and the separate unit tests (which ARE behavioral) are described as supplementary, not primary.
findings.location: Testing section — 'Characterization: the full reconcile suite … produces identical events/outcomes to pre-story `main` — the primary behavior-preserving guard'
scenarios: A pre-existing off-by-one in ADF truncation passes the characterization test (same wrong output) but the relocated logic silently inherits the bug
A lossy-status edge-case that was already wrong on main gets single-sourced into the adapter with the bug intact, invisible to the suite
findings.suggested_fix: For each relocated logic unit (ADF size-fitting, lossy status/parent, link constants): write unit tests against the documented Jira spec (e.g., 'ADF body ≤ 32767 bytes', 'lossy=True when status not in allowed-set'), not against current output. Keep the characterization suite as a non-regression harness but remove the word 'primary' from its description and ensure the unit tests assert spec-correct boundary values.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how `outbound_links` will import the single-sourced link constant from `adapters/jira/` in standalone-load test contexts (where `spec_from_file_location` provides no package context) — either by moving the constant to a package-context-free location (e.g. a new `rebar_reconciler/link_vocab.py`), using `lazy_load` to load the adapter module, or documenting that the standalone-load test pattern for `outbound_links` will be retired as part of this story.
evidence: outbound_links.py:30-35: 'the differ is loaded standalone via spec_from_file_location in tests (no package context, so `from rebar_reconciler.acli_graph import ...` is not reliably importable and would pull the whole ACLI client import chain). We re-declare a local copy here'

The plan says the constant will be 'single-sourced under adapters/jira/' and 'grepping the core sites for those constructs returns zero hits' — but provides no mechanism for how outbound_links.py will import it under standalone-load conditions
ABSENCE: no mention of how the standalone-load importability problem (which caused the duplication) is resolved when the source moves to adapters/jira/
findings.finding: The plan's mechanism for single-sourcing `outbound_links._RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK` into `adapters/jira/` ignores the documented reason the copy exists: `outbound_links.py` is loaded standalone via `spec_from_file_location` in tests (no package context), making `from rebar_reconciler.adapters.jira import ...` unreliable from that load context — the same importability constraint that caused the copy in the first place (per `outbound_links.py:30-35`). Moving the constant to `adapters/jira/` without solving the standalone-load importability problem would either break existing tests or require wrapping the import in the same `try/except` bootstrap pattern that `lazy_load` provides — and lazy_load was designed to load modules, not individual constants.
findings.location: Approach section / Scope section — 'Relocate ADF-sizing, lossy status/parent, duplicated value-maps, and the outbound_links link-constant copy into adapters/jira/'
scenarios: outbound_links.py is loaded via spec_from_file_location in tests without package context; importing from adapters.jira fails; tests break
The implementer adds a try/except bootstrap for the constant but now has a new fragile import pattern instead of a clean single-source
findings.suggested_fix: Move the link-type constant to a new package-internal module (e.g. `rebar_reconciler/_link_vocab.py`) that carries no Jira-specific import chain — then `adapters/jira/` can import FROM it (it owns the Jira interpretation layer), and `outbound_links.py` can also import it safely even via standalone load. This keeps it single-sourced without the adapter-importability problem.

findings.finding: The plan replaces hardcoded `"jira"` provider/creation-channel literals in `apply_inbound_records.py` with `RemoteRef`/`IdentityConvention`, but does not assert that these constructs resolve to the same runtime string value as the literal `"jira"`. If the runtime value differs, records written after cutover will carry a different provider identifier than records written before, creating a split persisted format with no backfill plan for pre-existing records — and code that filters or matches on the provider field may silently mis-classify old records.
findings.location: Scope section — 'Replace the apply_inbound_records.py "jira" literals with RemoteRef/IdentityConvention'; AC line 3
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence such as: 'RemoteRef("jira") and IdentityConvention.JIRA resolve to the same serialised string "jira" as the replaced literal; no existing persisted records require backfill and rollback simply reverts the constant usage.' If the value does differ, add an expand-contract migration: (1) dual-write both values during a transition window, (2) backfill old records in batches, (3) enforce the new value only after all reads are updated.

findings.finding: `run_differs.py:403` is listed as a '4 transport/construction sites' entry but uses a bare `from rebar_reconciler import acli as acli_mod_for_comments` import — there is no `_load_acli` seam in `run_differs.py` to evolve. The plan's mechanism ('update the `_load_acli` seams') cannot apply here without first adding a new seam, which is a distinct implementation step the plan omits.
findings.location: Context section, 'Core transport/construction sites' bullet 3: `run_differs.py:403`; Approach section: 'obtain the client from select_backend(config)'s TicketTransport and update the _load_acli seams'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit AC sub-item for _attestation.py: state that build_acli_client_from_env is routed through select_backend(config).transport and covered by a test (mirroring the run_differs treatment in AC4).
evidence: Context: '_attestation.py:82 — via build_acli_client_from_env (_attestation.py:41): route through the backend transport; update its construction path.'
Approach: 'For _attestation, route build_acli_client_from_env through the backend transport likewise.'

AC4 names only: 'existing applier/fetcher _load_acli seams and their 23 patch.object tests' and 'run_differs.py:403 ... new seam/transport-injection point ... new test' — _attestation.py not mentioned.
ABSENCE: no AC sub-item for _attestation seam change or its test coverage.
findings.finding: _attestation.py's transport routing (route build_acli_client_from_env through backend transport) is described in Context and Approach but AC4 only explicitly names applier/fetcher seams and run_differs — _attestation.py has no dedicated AC verification step. AC1's grep catches import removal but does not verify that the routing through select_backend(config).transport is correct and tested.
findings.location: Context (_attestation.py:82 bullet); Approach (last sentence of transport section); AC4
scenarios: _attestation.py routing is implemented but untested because no AC required a test for it — a regression at that site goes undetected.
Reviewer accepts the story as complete (all ACs green) without checking _attestation.py correctness.
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC4: 'and _attestation.py's build_acli_client_from_env is routed through select_backend(config).transport with a test that patches the backend transport construction — verified by make test.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `select_backend(config).transport` acquisition in `run_differs.py` and `_attestation.py` (and the evolved `_load_acli` seams) emits a structured log/metric on failure (e.g., `logger.error("backend transport acquisition failed", backend=..., exc_info=True)`) so operators can distinguish registry/config failures from network failures.
evidence: Plan is silent on error handling or instrumentation for the new `select_backend(config).transport` call path
ABSENCE: no mention of logging, metrics, alerting, or graceful degradation if `select_backend` or `.transport` raises in `run_differs` or `_attestation`

The existing bare `AcliClient(...)` construction also had no error handling, but the new indirection through a registry lookup is a new failure point (config mismatch, backend not registered) that didn't exist before
findings.finding: The new `select_backend(config).transport` routing paths introduced in `run_differs.py:403` and `_attestation.py:82` (and the evolved `_load_acli` seams in `applier`/`fetcher`) are not specified to emit any log, metric, or trace when transport acquisition fails. If `select_backend` raises (misconfigured backend, unregistered provider, missing transport) the caller gets an unhandled exception with no operator-visible signal, making production triage of the new routing layer impossible.
findings.location: Approach section (run_differs.py:403 and _attestation.py:82 transport routing); Testing section (no observability assertions)
scenarios: Backend is misconfigured (wrong provider key) — `select_backend` raises a `KeyError`; `run_differs` crashes with a stack trace but no log event operators can alert on
`_attestation.py` transport acquisition fails silently in a CI environment where the backend registry isn't seeded, giving a cryptic traceback with no actionable context
findings.suggested_fix: In the Approach section, add a sub-bullet under each new seam site: wrap `select_backend(config).transport` in a try/except that logs a structured error (`logger.error("transport_acquisition_failed", site=<module>, backend=config.backend, exc_info=True)`) before re-raising, and add an assertion in the 'Teeth' test that this log line appears when the backend is misconfigured.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
